title_en: What It’s Like Watching Dozens of Bodies Decompose (for Science)
title_zh: 科学观察几十具尸体腐败的真实样子

paragraphs.en: Somewhere out in the countryside, hidden behind a copse of trees, are fields full of dead human bodies. These corpses have been strategically laid out in rows, naked as the day they were born, and left to the mercy of the elements until all that’s left of them are bones.
paragraphs.zh: 在某个偏僻的乡村里，躲在一片树丛之后的地方，有一些埋着众多死者的土地。这些尸体被按行有序摆放，像刚出生时一样赤裸裸地暴露着，任由自然环境吞噬，直到只剩下骨头。

paragraphs.en: It sounds like a scene out of a horror film, but these places are real. They’re called taphonomic research facilities, or sometimes “body farms”—sites where forensic scientists study how the human body decomposes. (Don’t worry, the bodies are all donated.) By observing how fast cadavers break down in a controlled setting, investigators can learn more about decomposition and better pinpoint exactly what happened to dead bodies that are found in the real world.
paragraphs.zh: 听起来像是恐怖片里的场景，但这些地方是真实存在的。它们叫做“验尸环境学研究设施”，有时也被称为“尸体农场”——法医科学家在这里研究人体如何腐烂。（别担心，所有尸体都来自捐献者。）研究人员通过观察尸体在可控环境中的分解速度，能够更深入理解腐败过程，并更准确地判断现实世界中发现的尸体究竟经历了什么。

paragraphs.en: There are only a handful of body farms in existence, and most are in the US. Employees spend their days answering emails, cleaning bones, and leaving corpses out in the sun. WIRED spoke to one researcher and instructor in the US about their job—the good, the gross, and the pungent.
paragraphs.zh: 全世界真正的“尸体农场”寥寥无几，而且大多位于美国。工作人员的日常工作包括处理邮件、清理骨骼，以及把尸体晾在阳光下。WIRED采访了美国的一位研究人员兼讲师，了解了他们的工作——其中有有趣的、有恶心的，也有刺鼻的。

paragraphs.en: It makes me laugh on TV shows where they’re like, “Oh, well, this body was here for exactly three months.” Decomposition is such an individualized process for each donor. It depends on the person’s size, were they taking illicit drugs, were they undergoing chemo-therapy or radiation at the time? Cancer treatments will limit certain scavengers coming to the body, because those remains are going to smell different to those animals. I have placed donors next to each other at the same time, who could have died within days of each other, and one is going to skeletonize faster than the other. One might mummify. It is just such an individual process. Each donor teaches us something different about decomposition, contributing to our understanding of how the body breaks down with time, seasonality, temperature, and body composition. But that doesn’t make good TV.
paragraphs.zh: 我每次看电视剧时都会笑他们这么说：“啊，这具尸体就在这里好几个月了。”因为每个捐献者的腐败过程都非常不同。它取决于这个人的体型、是否使用过非法药物、是否当时正在接受化疗或放疗。癌症治疗会让某些清理腐败遗骸的动物不愿靠近，因为那具遗体的气味会与平常不一样。我曾经把几名捐献者在同一时间并排放置，按理说死亡时间只差几天，却会看到有人比别人更快骨化。有人会木乃伊化。这就是一个高度个体化的过程。每一位捐献者都会教给我们不同的分解知识，帮助我们理解人体在时间、季节、温度和身体成分等因素作用下如何分解。但这些细节可不是很好拍成电视剧。

paragraphs.en: We took more than 40 bodies into our care last year, and more than 50 in 2023. But more typical for us is 20 to 30 donors in a year. When a body arrives, we take photos, we take DNA swabs, if they consented to that when they were alive. And then we find a place for them.
paragraphs.zh: 去年我们接收了四十多个遗体，2023年接收了五十多个，但对我们来说更常见的是每年接收20到30位捐献者。遗体送达后，我们会拍照、采集DNA拭子（前提是其生前同意过）。然后，我们会为其安排安置。

paragraphs.en: Most of our donors will go out to our outdoor surface enclosure, where they are laid out unclothed, just on the ground. The enclosure follows the natural topography of the area and is double-fenced. We have some PVC and chicken-wire cages that we place over the remains at some point, to limit scavenging. We did recently have some turkey vultures that wiggled themselves under the cages and got caught. We also usually have several donors that we will bury in the natural soil within another enclosure. Those are only exhumed after several years, when they are expected to be skeletonized.
paragraphs.zh: 我们的多数捐献者会被放到户外的地表围场里，裸露放置在地面上。这个围场顺应场地的自然地形，并设置了双层围栏。我们有一些PVC和鸡网笼网，会在某个阶段架在遗体上方，以减少掠食者的干扰。最近我们发现有几只火鸡秃鹰钻进笼网下面并被困住。我们通常还会有几具捐献者被埋在另一个围场内的天然土壤中。这些遗体要经过多年后才会被出土，通常是在其预计已基本成为骨架之后。

paragraphs.en: We get drivers that are bringing donors to us that are like, “Oh, who are all these ladies?” We are not here for you to ogle at, we are scientists!
paragraphs.zh: 会有送遗体来的人对我们说："哦，这些都是什么样的女士？" 其实我们不是来让你观看的，我们是科学工作者。

paragraphs.en: We run classes at least twice a year, for our law enforcement and fire investigator partners. Donors who have consented to trauma research will be placed in a room that is set alight. We’ll let the donors cool for two days, and then the investigators practice moving a body to look for evidence that might have been shielded under a body and preserved. We also track the damage to the bodies, like how bones broke, and that can be really helpful for crime scene investigations.
paragraphs.zh: 我们每年至少为执法人员和火灾调查合作方开设两次课程。已同意参与创伤研究的捐献者会被放进一个被点燃的房间。我们会先让遗体冷却两天，然后让调查人员训练如何在搬动遗体时寻找可能被身体遮挡、却得以保存的证据。我们还会追踪遗体受损情况，比如骨折方式，这对犯罪现场调查通常非常有帮助。

paragraphs.en: Forensic anthropology in the US is becoming more female-dominated. Most of our students are female. Those of us running these facilities are mostly female. It is probably like a 9:1 ratio of women to men amongst our students here. We get drivers that are bringing donors to us that are like, “Oh, who are all these ladies?” We are not here for you to ogle at, we are scientists!
paragraphs.zh: 美国的法医人类学正变得越来越女性主导。我们大多数学生都是女性，管理这些设施的人也大多是女性。这里的学生男女比例大概是九比一。接送供体来的司机有时会问：“这些都是女士吗？”我们不是来给你看的，我们是科学研究人员！

paragraphs.en: We are always checking in with our students, because sometimes it’s hard to see a person go through that decomposition process. Or, when we get a new donor, we don’t know necessarily what we’ll find when we remove that sheet or open that body bag. I’ve only had one student who changed majors after being at our facility, though. Most of them thought they’d be the ones puking or passing out, and they’re not.
paragraphs.zh: 我们总是在和学生保持沟通，因为有时看着一个人经历腐烂过程实在太难受了。或者当我们迎来一位新供体时，也不一定知道掀开那块布或打开那具遗体袋时会看到什么。唯一一位在我们这里后改了专业的学生，我只遇到过一位。大多数人本以为自己会呕吐或昏倒，但实际上并不是。

paragraphs.en: You can love what you do and still realize that the smell of decomposing bodies is not great. There’s almost this sweetish smell to it, but it is very pungent. You can differentiate human decomposition from animal decomposition, when you get more familiar.
paragraphs.zh: 你可以热爱自己的工作，同时也清楚地意识到，腐烂遗体的气味并不好闻。它有一种几乎是甜味的味道，但非常刺鼻。随着经验增加，你能分辨出人体腐烂和动物腐烂之间的气味差异。

paragraphs.en: I would choose to donate my body. It’s kind of exciting in a way: You get to live forever.
paragraphs.zh: 我会选择捐献自己的身体。这种想法有点令人激动：这样你就能永生了。

paragraphs.en: The hardest part of this job, though, is that you have to talk to family members who either just lost a loved one or are actively losing a loved one. We often speak to donors’ families multiple times: before or after a donor has passed, as well as in the time after a donor is in our care. There are several families that check in regularly or even visit their loved ones in the skeletal collection. Sometimes you cry with them, you laugh with them, and then you have to go off and teach a class, or there’s another phone call or an email. You have to be able to compartmentalize. That can be a little difficult.
paragraphs.zh: 这份工作的最艰难部分是，你必须与刚失去亲人或正经历失去的亲人的家属交谈。我们通常会与捐献者的家属多次沟通：在捐献者去世前或去世后，以及在捐献者进入我们照护阶段之后。有些家庭会定期来电询问，甚至会到骨骼收藏室看望亲人。有时你会和他们一起哭，也会和他们一起笑，然后你得离开去上一堂课，或者接另一个电话，或处理一封邮件。你必须学会把这些情绪分开。这有点难。

paragraphs.en: We understand that this job relies on the generosity and trust of other people, and that is something that we always try to instill in our students. It’s a privilege, and it’s not something we take for granted.
paragraphs.zh: 我们明白这项工作依赖于他人的慷慨与信任，这也是我们始终努力在学生中培养的。它是一份特权，我们绝不应该把它当成理所当然。

paragraphs.en: I would choose to donate my body. It’s kind of exciting in a way: You get to live forever. Some of the pre-donors I talk to, that’s what’s really exciting for them, too. They kind of have a life afterward, after they’re done with their bodies. I don’t want to be cremated. I don’t want to be embalmed and shoved in the ground somewhere. I would much rather my corporealness go off and be put to good use somewhere.
paragraphs.zh: 我会选择捐献自己的身体。这在某种意义上让我觉得很激动：你可以永远留下来。有些预捐者和我谈起这点时，也同样觉得这很激动人心。这样一来，他们在身体完成了使命之后，就像又有了一种新的生命。我不想被火化，不想被防腐后埋在某个地方。我更愿意让自己的身体离开之后在某处被充分利用。
